1:
The witnesses agree on the bare wonder — corn dirt-cheap within a day in a starving city — and divide their labour between the mechanics of the promise and its theology. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott protests that the chapter division is unfortunate, for there is no break in the story: Elisha's word answers directly the king's despairing outburst that closed the previous chapter (2 Kings 6:33), and the prophet addresses the king and his attendants. He restores the abruptness of the Hebrew — 'About this time to-morrow a seah of fine flour at a shekel, and two seahs of barley at a shekel, in the gate of Samaria!' — and explains the terms: a seah was the common corn measure (1 Kings 18:32; 2 Kings 6:25), barley served not only as fodder but as human food in cakes (Judges 7:13), and 'the gate' names the open market just within the walls (Gen. 18:6 supplies the fine flour). Adam Clarke reckons the prices to make the miracle vivid: a seah of fine flour for a shekel, some two gallons and a half for little more than two shillings — a world away from the famine price of an ass's head in the chapter before. John Wesley simply glosses the seah as about a peck and a pottle.

The reformed John Gill notes tersely that Elisha spoke this 'to the king and those that were with him,' turning the prophet's word to its proper audience. It is the puritan Matthew Henry who mines the verse for its doctrine. He reads the timing as the very signature of grace: the king had grown weary of waiting and cried, 'What should I wait for the Lord any longer?' — and precisely then, when things were at their worst, the word came. 'Man's extremity is God's opportunity of magnifying his own power; his time to appear for his people is when their strength is gone' (Deut. 32:36). Henry hears in the promise of a reopened market the very language of restored peace (Judg. 5:11), and marvels that though the king had just threatened Elisha's life, God answers with abundance — 'where sin abounded, grace doth much more abound.' He even glances at the Gospel: when the Son of Man comes, shall he find faith on the earth? (Luke 18:8).

So the schools do not contend here; they divide the work. The grammatical-historical men weigh the seah and the shekel and locate the market in the gate; the reformed and puritan voices lift the cheap corn into a parable of a God who waits until human hope is spent and then, unasked and against all deserving, opens his hand. The one shadow — the scoffing captain of v.2 — is already in view, for Elisha speaks his promise to a court that will not all believe it.
2:
Here the witnesses find their one real quarrel, and it concerns not the words but the death. All agree on the scene. The grammatical-historical Ellicott calls the shalish an adjutant or aide-de-camp, an esquire on whose hand the king leaned (comparing the like posture toward Naaman's master, 2 Kings 5:18), and Gill lingers on the state of it — a principal lord, 'the third to the king,' on whose arm the monarch literally rested, as Persian queens leaned upon their maidens. His scoff is unmistakable: even if the Lord were to make 'windows in heaven' (Gen. 7:11) and rain down grain, could such plenty come so soon? Ellicott catches the sneer — 'the tone is that of scoffing unbelief' — and notes that Elisha's reply pointedly imitates the scoffer's own opening: 'Behold, thou art destined to see it with thine eyes, but shalt not eat thereof' (compare 2 Kings 5:26).

The division opens over the meaning of that sentence. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke insists it is 'a mere prediction of his death, but not as a judgment for his unbelief.' Anyone in his circumstances, Clarke argues, might have said the same, for nothing short of a miracle could produce such plenty; and by a miracle alone it was done. The man simply stated the plain improbability, and any officer in his place might have been trodden to death in the press at the gate — no special stroke of vengeance, but the ordinary hazard of a crowd. Against this stands the reading the puritan Matthew Henry makes explicit at the chapter's close: the doom is 'the just doom passed upon him for his infidelity.' For Henry the sentence is precisely a judgment — 'unbelief is a sin by which men greatly dishonour and displease God, and deprive themselves of the favours he designed for them' — and he likens the courtier to the murmuring Israelites who saw Canaan but could not enter because of unbelief. Gill stands nearer Henry, reading the whole exchange as bold unbelief rebuked, and dwells on the irony that so honoured a man should stumble at the very word that would have fed him.

The debate is genuine and instructive. Is the captain's fate a natural consequence — the accident of any man set in a stampeding gateway — or a signed judgment upon distrust of God's word? Clarke, ever cautious about reading providence too confidently, will not call it vengeance; Henry, reading the same event through the design of the whole narrative, calls it exactly that. Yet even here the schools converge on the moral: the man who limits the Holy One to 'windows in heaven' has already narrowed his own soul, and whether by judgment or by the mere outworking of events he will see the mercy he mocked and never taste it — a pattern of the God who acts when strength is gone (Deut. 32:36).
3:
The narrative turns from the palace to the gate, and the witnesses read the four lepers as the unlikeliest of God's messengers. The grammatical-historical Ellicott renders the Hebrew exactly — 'and four men were lepers' — and, with Keil & Delitzsch, grounds their station outside the gate in the law that shut the leper out of human society (Lev. 13:46; Num. 5:2). Keil observes that lepers still had their huts by the Zion gate at Jerusalem in his own day, and that these men sat 'separated from human society' in some booth erected for the purpose. Their question — 'Why sit we here until we die?' — Ellicott glosses as 'why are we abiding?', for in the pressure of the famine none brought them food any longer. John Gill agrees that the law kept them without the city, yet notes they clung as near the gate as they could, partly to beg relief and partly for fear of the enemy.

All the older witnesses pause on a rabbinic tradition. Gill, Clarke, and Ellicott alike report that the Gemara (in the tractate Sotah), with Rashi and Jarchi, identifies the four as Gehazi and his three sons — the servant struck with leprosy for his greed in the earlier story, now reduced to the very outcast state that his master had pronounced (2 Kings 5:26). Clarke adds the plain historical fact that underlies the whole scene: 'they were not permitted to mingle in civil society.'

The puritan Matthew Henry lifts the detail into providence. Samaria, he says, 'was delivered, and did not know it'; the watchmen on the walls never caught the enemy's silent retreat, so 'Providence employed four lepers to be the intelligencers.' There is a quiet irony the witnesses all feel: the men the city had cast out as unclean become the bearers of its salvation, and Gehazi — if he be among them — is providentially set where the king will afterward take notice of him (2 Kings 8:4). Excluded by the ceremonial law from the congregation, they are not excluded from the errand of grace; and the desperate arithmetic of their next words (v.4) will carry the deliverance of a nation. The voices do not divide here — from Rashi to Henry they agree that God chose the weakest and most despised to shame the strong and to feed the starving.
4:
This verse is the lepers' desperate syllogism, and the witnesses read it as a model of reasoning driven to God's mercy by sheer extremity. The grammatical-historical commentators lay the logic bare. Keil & Delitzsch set the three doors before them: to enter the city is to meet the famine and die; to sit still at the gate is to die; therefore only one course holds any hope — 'let us fall unto the host of the Syrians.' Ellicott sharpens the verb: 'fall unto' means to desert, to go over to the enemy; and if the Syrians 'save us alive,' it will be by giving food out of pity. He notes that the 'but' of 'we shall but die' is not in the Hebrew — the sense is starker still: death is certain in the city, certain at the gate, and only uncertain in the camp. Gill adds that to enter the city would be to break the law that barred them, so even that grim option was closed.

The puritan Matthew Henry turns their calculation into a small sermon on prudence and on dying. 'Common prudence,' he writes, 'will put us upon that method which may better our condition, but cannot make it worse.' If the Syrians kill them, they do but die — one death rather than a thousand, the sword rather than the slow torment of famine; but perhaps they will be spared as objects of compassion. Henry hears in it an echo of the prodigal, who resolved to return to a father he feared rather than perish with hunger in the far country: better to venture on mercy than to sit still and starve.

And Henry will not let the phrase 'we shall but die' pass without its evangelical weight. Happy, he says, are they who in another and higher sense can speak so of dying — 'we shall but die, that is the worst of it; not die and be damned, not be hurt of the second death.' The lepers reason only about the body, yet their words become, in the puritan's hands, the language of a soul that has made its peace beyond the grave. The witnesses do not contend; together they find in these four famine-wasted men a picture of faith's boldness — that when every door but one is shut, the venture upon undeserved mercy is the whole of wisdom, and the very resolution that follows in v.5 walks them straight into an abandoned camp.
5:
The witnesses treat this verse as the hinge of the whole deliverance, and they attend to its timing with care. The grammatical-historical Ellicott fixes the hour: 'in the twilight' means at nightfall, and he observes that the lepers deliberately waited until then so that their departure from the gate would not be marked from the walls (compare the notes of v.9 and v.12). Keil & Delitzsch agree it was the evening dusk, not the morning, and argue the point from the king's night-council that follows. Gill too glosses it as the dusk of the evening. When they reach 'the uttermost part of the camp,' Ellicott and Clarke note the phrase means the outskirts nearest to Samaria — the very verge where, as Clarke drily remarks, 'the Syrian advanced guards should have been.' And there was no man there.

The silence of that outpost is the marvel on which the schools converge. Gill and the others feel the sudden emptiness where a sentry ought to stand: the lepers, braced to throw themselves on an enemy's mercy, find no enemy at all. The puritan Matthew Henry draws out the providential exactness of the moment. The Syrians 'fled in the twilight, the evening twilight,' and 'in the twilight the lepers came' — so that, as Henry says, 'no time was lost.' Providence ordered it that these outcasts should arrive at the very instant the camp fell empty, neither so early as to meet the host nor so late as to let the news grow cold.

Beneath the topography the witnesses hear one lesson. The men came expecting to beg their lives; they found instead a deserted wealth, and the reader is meant to see that the deliverance was wholly God's, wrought while Samaria slept and its watchmen kept their useless guard. The empty tents of v.5 are the visible side of the invisible terror that v.6 will explain — a rout with no pursuer, a spoil with no battle, and four despised men standing where a whole army had fled. What looked to the lepers like a last, reckless gamble was in truth a table already spread; and the God who empties a camp in the dark is the God who had promised, a day before, to fill a market at the gate.
6:
This verse supplies the cause of the empty camp, and the witnesses divide their attention between how the terror was wrought and what the Syrians made of it. On the first point John Gill lays out the options without deciding: the Lord made the host 'hear a noise of chariots and horses' either by the ministry of angels in the air, or by so working upon their imagination that they fancied the sound, or by causing the noise directly in their ears. Ellicott, in a long note, leans to 'a sound in the air among the neighbouring hills,' observing that the Hebrew word for the noise is the same commonly used of thunder (as throughout Ps. 29). Clarke keeps to the effect: the sound threw the camp into confusion, for they imagined themselves about to be attacked by powerful auxiliaries whom the king of Israel had hired.

The puritan Matthew Henry makes the theology explicit: the siege was raised 'not by might or power, but by the Spirit of the Lord of hosts.' At Dothan God had imposed upon the enemy's sight (2 Kings 6:18); here he imposes upon their hearing, for 'he makes the hearing ear and the seeing eye' and knows how to work upon every sense (Exod. 4:11). 'There were they in great fear where no fear was.' The witnesses agree that the Syrians then reasoned themselves into a panic, concluding the king had hired 'the kings of the Hittites and the kings of the Egyptians' to fall upon them.

Over the identity of those kings a quieter difference appears. Ellicott presses the historical reality: the Hittites were a great northern power between the Euphrates and the Orontes, with capitals at Carchemish and Hamath, so that 'kings of the Hittites' names real rival princes, while 'kings of the Egyptians' is likely a rhetorical plural for the one Pharaoh. Wesley, by contrast, takes 'Hittites' loosely for the scattered peoples of Canaan who survived the conquest and settled on its borders, and reads the Egyptian plural either as the king of Egypt or his provincial governors. Ellicott adds a caution the others would grant: the narrative rests on oral tradition, and we need not press the exact wording of a panic no survivor stayed to record. Whether the sound was angelic or imagined, and whether the dreaded kings were the historic Hittite empire or a remnant of Canaan, the witnesses are one on the substance — God alone dispersed the host, striking terror into hearts that no sword had touched, so that the flight of v.7 follows of course.
7:
The flight itself the witnesses read as a study in God-given cowardice. The grammatical-historical Ellicott paints the scene from the Hebrew: the verse 'gives a vivid picture of a wild flight, in which everything was forgotten except personal safety.' They rose and fled 'in the twilight,' abandoning tents, horses, and asses — the camp left standing 'as it was' — and ran 'for their life.' Gill notes from the Targum that it was in the dark, and that the lepers came upon the deserted camp very soon after, so closely did the two movements follow.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley fastens on the folly of the rout: 'None of them had so much sense as to send scouts to discover the supposed enemy, much less courage enough to face them.' Here he and the puritan Matthew Henry speak as with one voice, both reaching for the same Scripture — 'the wicked flee when none pursueth.' 'God can, when he pleases,' writes Henry, 'dispirit the boldest and most brave, and make the stoutest heart to tremble'; 'those that will not fear God he can make to fear at the shaking of a leaf.' Wesley echoes the very phrase almost word for word, so that the panic of a whole army becomes a proof that men who reject the fear of God are left defenceless before phantom terrors.

Wesley adds one antiquarian aside that the witnesses have carried from verse to verse: 'perhaps Gehazi was one of these lepers, which might occasion his being taken notice of by the king' (2 Kings 8:4). But the weight of the verse lies elsewhere. The horses that might have sped their flight they could not stay to take; the very means of escape were left behind, so headlong was the fear. The witnesses do not divide; they agree that the deliverance of Samaria cost Israel not a single blow, that the mightiest host melts when God withdraws its courage, and that the abandoned tents now stood ready — a spoil without a battle — for the four outcasts who were even then stealing into the camp. What the besiegers fled from was nothing; what they fled toward was ruin; and what they left behind became, by the word spoken in v.1, the salvation of the city they had meant to starve.
8:
This verse the witnesses read as the pivot between a private windfall and a public duty — the moment hunger is satisfied and the heart must choose. The grammatical-historical Ellicott observes that the narrative here 'takes up the thread again' from the point where it broke off to explain the Syrians' flight: 'those lepers came to the edge of the camp, and they went into one tent.' Gill adds only that it was the first tent they reached. There, famished men that they were, they ate and drank; then they carried off silver and gold and raiment 'and went and hid it,' and returned and plundered another tent and hid its spoil as well. Ellicott notes that this hiding of treasure in holes in the ground or the tent-wall was 'a common practice of Orientals, with whom holes in the ground or in the house wall supply the place of banks.'

The witnesses feel the human truth of the scene without moralizing it harshly. The lepers do exactly what starving and destitute men would do: first they fill the belly that had driven them to this desperate errand, and then, finding themselves suddenly rich, they begin to provide for a future they had an hour before despaired of. The instinct is natural, even innocent as far as it goes; but it is also the instinct of self, and the reader is meant to watch it running its course until conscience checks it.

For the very fullness of the tents raises the question the next verse will press. Men who came expecting to beg their lives, or to be killed, now sit amid more wealth than they can carry, hoarding it while a whole city starves behind the walls. The grammatical-historical commentators are content to record the custom and the sequence; but their plain narration sets the stage for the turn of heart in v.9, where these same men, glutted and enriched, will say to one another, 'We do not well.' The buried silver of v.8 is the measure of the temptation; the good tidings they are sitting on are worth more than all of it, and the deliverance God has wrought is not meant to be hidden in a hole with the plunder. Thus even in its quiet, factual detail the verse prepares the great question of whether mercy received may be selfishly kept.
9:
On this verse the witnesses reach the moral summit of the chapter, and they read the lepers' change of mind as the very logic of the gospel. The grammatical-historical commentators secure the words. Keil & Delitzsch give the resolution in full: 'this day is a day of good tidings, and we hold our peace; if we tarry till the morning light, some mischief will come upon us.' Ellicott renders the warning more literally — 'guilt will find us; we shall incur blame,' as the Vulgate has it, 'we shall be accused of wrong-doing' — and marks the inferential 'now therefore' with which they rouse themselves to act. Gill catches the plain self-reproach: it is 'not right, to take this booty to ourselves; it is not doing justice to our brethren, and it may not prove well to ourselves in the issue.'

The puritan Matthew Henry makes the verse a sermon on evangelism. The lepers 'corrected themselves,' he says, refusing to conceal the good news 'under colour of being avenged upon' the community that had shut them out — for it was the law, not their neighbours, that had excluded them. Their own consciences told them that mischief would befall them if they acted 'separately, and sought themselves only,' and Henry draws the general rule: 'selfish, narrow-spirited people cannot expect to prosper; the most comfortable advantage is that which our brethren share with us in.' Then he lifts the whole scene into the light of Christ: 'they who have found the unsearchable riches of Christ will not long delay to report the good tidings to others'; from love to him, 'not from selfish feelings, they will gladly share their earthly good things with their brethren.'

The witnesses do not divide here; they converge in one confession. The natural checks that the reformed and grammatical-historical voices name — the fear of blame, the prick of conscience — are, for Henry, the shadow of a higher constraint: the man who has tasted mercy is under obligation to proclaim it. A famine-city sleeps behind the walls while four outcasts sit amid a spoil that could feed it; and the verse turns on the refusal to hoard grace. It is the very posture of the church that has found in Christ a treasure it dare not keep to itself — a day of good tidings is no day for silence — and so the lepers rise and go to the gate (v.10), carrying to a doomed city the news of a deliverance they did not earn.
10:
With the good tidings resolved upon, the narrative follows its careful chain of report, and here the grammatical-historical commentators do almost all the work. Gill notes that the lepers 'called to the porter of the city,' the chief of those who kept the gate, for there was more than one. Ellicott weighs the word closely: the singular 'porter' is probably collective, since 'they told them' immediately follows and the plural 'porters' appears in the next verse; the Septuagint and Vulgate, reading the same consonants differently, render 'gate,' which attests the antiquity of the text. He also draws out the precision of the lepers' message: 'there was no man there, neither voice of man' — the first word denotes an individual man, the second the species, and so 'includes women and children.' The horses and asses stand 'tied,' that is tethered and feeding, and the tents remain 'as they were.'

The report is, on its face, a plain piece of eyewitness testimony, and the witnesses honour it as such. The lepers do not exaggerate or theorize; they describe exactly what they saw — an army's whole encampment intact and utterly abandoned, not a human voice within it, the very beasts left standing at their pickets. It is the language of men reporting a marvel too strange to embellish, and the guarded, itemized detail (tied horses, tied asses, silent tents) carries its own credibility.

Beneath the topographical and textual notes the witnesses feel the quiet irony that has run through the chapter. The unclean men whom the city had thrust outside its gate now stand at that same gate as heralds of its rescue, and the first ears to receive the news of salvation are the watchmen's. The message must climb from the outcast to the porter, from the porter to the palace; and the grammatical-historical reading, for all its care over collectives and variant readings, serves a larger point — that God's deliverance, wrought in secret and reported by the despised, comes to a sleeping city through the humblest of channels. The porters will carry it inward (v.11), and the king himself must be roused to reckon with tidings first brought by lepers.
11:
This brief verse traces the news one step further inward, and the witnesses treat it chiefly as a matter of accurate reporting. Gill reads it straightforwardly: the porter of the city called to the porters of the king's palace, who carried the tidings to the household within. Ellicott, attending to the grammar, prefers 'and the porters called,' noting that the Hebrew verb is singular and may be impersonal — 'and one called, namely the warders' — though the Septuagint, Targum, and several Hebrew manuscripts read the plural. He infers from the word 'within' that the king's palace stood near the ramparts, so that the sentries at the gate could shout their news to soldiers near them, who conveyed it to the palace; and he observes that the Authorized Version's rendering cannot be quite right, since the Hebrew idiom would otherwise require a different preposition.

For all the smallness of the verse, the witnesses feel the movement it records. The report ascends by stages — outcast lepers to the city porter, the city porter to the palace porters, the palace porters to the king's house — a whole apparatus of watch and ward set in motion by four men whom that same establishment had shut outside its walls. The very officers whose business was to keep the unclean out now become the relays that carry the unclean men's good news to the throne.

And the tidings lose nothing by their lowly origin. As Matthew Henry observed of the whole episode, the intelligence 'was not the less acceptable for being first brought by lepers.' The chapter has been quietly insisting that God delights to bring his greatest mercies through the meanest instruments, and this verse is one more link in that argument: the salvation of Samaria, announced a day before by a prophet and wrought in the dark by an unseen terror, now travels up through the city's own gatekeepers to reach a king who will greet it, at first, not with faith but with suspicion (v.12). The word climbs faithfully to the palace; whether it will be believed there is another matter.
12:
The king's reaction turns the witnesses to the question of suspicion, and they read his midnight council in two minds. The narrative is agreed: roused in the night, Jehoram tells his servants he will 'shew them what the Syrians have done to us' — knowing Israel to be starving, the enemy has feigned flight and hidden in the field, meaning to seize the city's people as they come out for spoil and so break in. Keil & Delitzsch put it plainly: the king imagined the unexpected departure was only a ruse.

Here the schools part company over how to judge him. The grammatical-historical and wesleyan-arminian voices are inclined to excuse the caution. Ellicott quotes Grotius — 'suspicax est miseria,' misery is suspicious — and notes that such stratagems as Jehoram feared 'are common enough in warfare.' Clarke goes further in the king's defence: 'this king had made a noble defence; he seems to have shared in all the sufferings of the besieged, and to have been ever at his post. Even in vile Ahab there were some good things.' On this reading the king's wariness is only prudent generalship — the very stratagem he dreaded was one the Syrians might well have used, since they knew the city was hungry.

The puritan Matthew Henry reads the same suspicion as the fruit of a bad conscience. Jehoram 'knew he had no reason to expect that God should appear thus wonderfully for him, having forfeited his favour by his unbelief and impatience'; and so, Henry concludes, 'a guilty conscience fears the worst and makes men suspicious.' 'Let not those who, like him, are unstable in all their ways, think to receive any thing from God.' For Henry the king's failure to believe the good news is of a piece with his despairing cry of the previous chapter (2 Kings 6:33): the man who would not wait for the Lord cannot now credit the Lord's deliverance. The debate is real — is this the sober prudence of a tried commander (Clarke, Ellicott) or the restless dread of an unbelieving heart (Henry)? — yet both sides grant the outcome: the king will not act on the report until it is proved, and so he sends out scouts (v.13) to test a mercy he cannot bring himself to trust.
13:
The counsel to send scouts hangs on one of the most vexed sentences in the book, and the witnesses divide precisely over its tangled text. All agree on the substance: a servant proposes to take some of the surviving horses and send men to learn what has become of the Syrians. Ellicott notes that 'five' is used, like our 'half a dozen,' for a small indefinite number (Lev. 26:8; Isa. 30:17), though only two pairs were actually taken (v.14). Keil & Delitzsch simply record the advice and its issue.

The knot is the doubled clause about the horses being 'as all the multitude of Israel.' Clarke judges it an outright scribal blunder: following Kennicott, he holds that seven Hebrew words were carelessly repeated by a copyist — a dittography absent from the Septuagint and Syriac and from more than forty manuscripts — and that the verse should be read without the repetition. He adds that the phrase rendered 'are consumed' really means 'which are perfect,' that is, fit for service, the rest of the horses having died or been eaten in the famine. Ellicott, by contrast, defends the received text and finds sense in the very repetition: it 'dwells pathetically on the fewness of those that survive,' the servant weighing two outcomes — the scouts may return to share the fate of 'all the multitude of Israel that are left,' who yet may die of famine, or be caught and slain like the multitude already 'consumed.' Either way, as at v.4, nothing worse can befall the men sent than has befallen the rest.

Wesley takes yet a third path through the words. He would not read the Hebrew particle as 'like' at all but as an affirmation of certainty — 'behold, they are of a truth all the multitude of the horses of Israel that are left' — so that the clause is 'worthy of a double behold,' marking how the famine had wasted man and beast alike and shrunk the king's whole cavalry to these few. Gill keeps to the plain sense of horses that had not died in the dearth. The disagreement is real but bounded, and it is a scholar's disagreement over a corrupt and difficult verse: whether the doubling is a copyist's slip to be removed (Clarke), a pathetic emphasis to be kept (Ellicott), or an affirmation of certainty to be re-pointed (Wesley). Beneath the dispute all agree on the picture — a kingdom so reduced that its scouting party must be scraped together from a handful of starving horses.
14:
This verse records the sending out of the reconnaissance, and the witnesses attend mainly to a small discrepancy of number and the prudence of the plan. Gill notes that they took 'two chariot horses' only, not the five proposed, and supposes them the best-fed beasts, perhaps the king's own team, and so the fittest for the errand. Clarke reconciles the counsel and the deed: they had at first intended to send five, but 'probably they found on examination that only two were effective'; or, if two chariots were sent, each would have its pair, with perhaps a single horse besides for crossing the country. Ellicott reads the Hebrew as 'two chariots of horses,' that is two teams, the chariots and their drivers implied rather than named.

Ellicott adds the tactical sense of it: two chariots were sent 'so that if attacked they might make a better resistance,' or 'in order that, if one were captured by the enemy, the other might escape with the news.' It is the arrangement of men who half expect a trap — the very suspicion the king voiced in the verse before (v.12) — and it shows the deliverance being tested with cautious, soldierly care rather than seized on trust.

The witnesses do not divide here; the interest is wholly in the concrete detail, and the detail quietly reinforces the chapter's larger theme. A city that a day before could muster no relief now sends out its last serviceable horses to chase a phantom army, and the reader already knows what the scouts will find. The king's deliberate, distrustful probing — send two teams, keep one in reserve, be ready to fight or flee — stands in silent contrast to the prophet's flat assurance in v.1 that by this hour tomorrow the famine would be over. The chariots roll out to verify a mercy that was never in doubt, and their track will lead, in v.15, along a road strewn with the litter of a rout that no Israelite hand had caused. The witnesses leave the theology aside on this verse and simply mark the soldier's carefulness — how many horses, how many chariots, and to what end — content to let the concrete facts speak. Yet the very smallness of the detail carries the argument forward: a nation stripped by famine to two serviceable teams is about to be enriched with the whole spoil of the army that besieged it, and the men who ride out in fear will come home with news that turns dread into deliverance.
15:
The scouts' pursuit confirms the rout, and the witnesses read the strewn road as the very signature of panic. Gill notes that, not finding the Syrians in the camp and knowing the route they must take homeward, the messengers followed 'as far as Jordan,' the river they would have to cross. Ellicott qualifies the geography: 'unto Jordan' means in the direction of the river, not the whole twenty miles to its bank. Along the way they found the road 'full of garments and vessels, which the Syrians had cast away in their haste' — and Ellicott gathers the parallels that fix the sense of that word for headlong flight (1 Sam. 23:26; Ps. 48:5; Ps. 104:7), showing the received text right against the margin.

Clarke reads the debris for what it proves: the abandoned garments and vessels are 'a manifest proof of the hurry and precipitancy with which they fled.' Men do not shed their clothing and drop their gear except in terror; the very litter of the road testifies that the host had run for its life, exactly as the previous verse described. The messengers 'returned, and told the king,' and the report was now beyond doubting.

The witnesses do not contend over so plain a scene, and its force is cumulative. The chapter has shown a besieging army melting away at a sound in the night, its tents left standing, its beasts tethered; now it adds the trail of jettisoned spoil stretching toward the Jordan, as if to spell out for the doubting king that the deliverance was total and the enemy truly gone. There is no ambush, no reserve lying in the field — only the wreckage of flight. The scouts who set out to test a mercy return as its confirming witnesses; and with the report verified, the way is open for the people of Samaria to pour out and plunder the camp, so that the prophet's word of cheap corn at the gate (v.1) passes at last from promise into fulfilment in v.16. The king's distrust is answered not by argument but by the plain evidence of the road, and the same providence that emptied the camp now furnishes the proof that will empty it of its spoil. Every cast-off cloak and abandoned vessel is a silent witness that God had already done what the prophet promised, and that the doubting court had only to go and gather what grace had won.
16:
The fulfilment arrives, and the witnesses read it as the vindication of a word that could not fail. Gill records the plain event: 'the people went out and spoiled the tents of the Syrians,' seizing riches and provisions in such abundance that there was plenty not only for present need but for sale, so that a measure of fine flour was sold for a shekel and two of barley for a shekel, 'according to the word of the Lord.' Keil & Delitzsch note that the plundering of the camp was the immediate cause of the cheapness Elisha had foretold, and Ellicott attends to the phrasing, reading 'the camp' and the solemn 'and it came to pass' with which the prophecy is sealed.

The puritan Matthew Henry gathers the harvest of doctrine. He sees here 'the wealth of the sinner laid up for the just' (Job 27:16) and 'the spoilers spoiled' (Isa. 33:1): the very siege meant for Samaria's ruin is turned to its enrichment, and 'Israel is now enriched with the spoil of the Syrians as of old with that of the Egyptians.' He marvels that the wants of Israel were 'supplied in a way that they little thought of,' and finds even the stay-at-homes sharing in the plunder (Ps. 68:12; Isa. 33:23). Above all he presses the exactness of the prophecy: 'the word of Elisha was fulfilled to a tittle... God's promise may be safely relied on, for no word of his shall fall to the ground.'

The witnesses do not divide; they close ranks around the reliability of God's word. The reformed and grammatical-historical voices trace the plain mechanics — a deserted camp, a rush to spoil it, a market suddenly glutted — while the puritan lifts those mechanics into a testimony that heaven keeps its promises to the letter. A day before, the price named in v.1 had seemed impossible even to a king's counsellor; now it is the going rate at the gate. And the same fulfilment that feeds the city seals the doom of the man who mocked it, for the prophet had spoken a threat as well as a promise, and v.17 will show it, too, accomplished 'to a tittle.'
17:
The chapter's threat now falls due, and the witnesses read the courtier's death as the exact counterpart of the courtier's unbelief. Gill notes that the king set the lord 'on whose hand he leaned' over the gate — not to keep out an enemy, of whom there was now no danger, but to marshal the people and prevent disorder as they streamed out for spoil. It was a post of honour and trust. Clarke tells what came of it: 'the people rushing out to get spoil, and in to carry it to their houses, he was borne down by the multitude and trodden to death.' This, he adds, 'was foreseen by the spirit of prophecy,' and 'the literal and exact fulfilment of such predictions must have acquired the prophet a great deal of credit in Israel.' Keil & Delitzsch set verses seventeen through twenty side by side to show the prediction and the event matched word for word.

The puritan Matthew Henry weighs the manner of the death. The people trod him down 'either by accident, the crowd being exceedingly great,' or 'perhaps designedly, because he abused his power, and was imperious in restraining the people from satisfying their hunger.' However it fell out, 'God's justice was glorified, and the word of God was fulfilled.' The bitter irony Henry will not let pass is that the man 'saw the plenty, for the silencing and shaming of his unbelief... but he did not eat of the plenty he saw.' When he was about to fill his belly, judgment came 'between the cup and the lip' (Job 20:23).

The witnesses are of one mind on the lesson. 'Divine threatenings will as surely be accomplished as divine promises,' writes Henry; the same day's spoil that proved God's promise true proved his warning true as well. There is a grim symmetry the schools all feel: the officer on whose arm the king had leaned is trampled under the feet of the crowd, exalted honour and inglorious ruin meeting in one man. He had limited the Holy One to 'windows in heaven'; God gave the plenty without them and let the scoffer perish in the very abundance he had scorned. The reformed, grammatical-historical, and puritan voices differ only in emphasis — Clarke marking the prophet's vindicated credit, Henry the sinner's just doom — and both are set out at length in the deliberate replay of vv.18-20.
18:
The witnesses recognize that the narrator now doubles back to repeat the prophecy and its fulfilment, and they read the repetition as a deliberate stress upon the certainty of God's word. Keil & Delitzsch note that verses eighteen through twenty restate, almost verbatim, both Elisha's original promise and the scoffer's retort, so that prediction and event may be laid exactly side by side. The prophet had said that on the morrow two measures of barley would go for a shekel and a measure of fine flour for a shekel in the gate of Samaria; and so it had come to pass. The grammatical-historical reading treats the restatement not as idle duplication but as the historian's careful method — the word is quoted again beside its accomplishment precisely so that no reader can miss the correspondence.

The puritan Matthew Henry supplies the reason for the emphasis: 'this matter is repeated, and the event very particularly compared with the prediction, that we might take special notice of it.' The doubling is pastoral, not accidental; the Spirit lingers over the fulfilled word to fix its lesson in the heart. What had been promised in v.1, and had seemed to the king's counsellor a sheer impossibility, is now set down a second time as accomplished fact.

The witnesses do not divide over so plain a passage, and its force is cumulative. By reciting the promise again at the moment of its fulfilment, the narrative presses home the truth that has governed the whole chapter — that not one word of God shall fall to the ground. The plenty at the gate is no lucky turn of fortune but the exact discharge of a pledge given a day before against every appearance of possibility. And the same deliberate repetition that seals the promise is about to seal the threat, for the verse that quoted Elisha's word of abundance leads straight to the scoffer's answer in v.19 and to the fate that answer earned — so that reader and doubter alike are taught how surely both the mercies and the warnings of God arrive at their appointed hour.
19:
The narrator now repeats the scoffer's own words, and the witnesses read the replay as a fresh exposure of the sin of unbelief. The captain had answered the man of God, 'Behold, if the Lord should make windows in heaven, might such a thing be?' — and Elisha had said, 'Behold, thou shalt see it with thine eyes, but shalt not eat thereof.' Keil & Delitzsch set the retort down again beside the prophet's sentence, and the grammatical-historical reading marks how the phrase 'windows in heaven' fixes the man's whole error: he could conceive of plenty only by a repetition of the flood-gates opened at the deluge (Gen. 7:11), and would set the bounds of God's power by the narrow measure of his own imagining.

The puritan Matthew Henry draws from the repeated scoff the first of his great lessons on this chapter: it teaches 'how deeply God resents our distrust of him, of his power, providence, and promise.' He recalls Israel in the wilderness, who asked, 'Can God furnish a table?' — 'the Lord heard it, and was wroth' (Ps. 78:19) — and lays down the maxim that answers the courtier's sneer: 'Infinite wisdom will not be limited by our folly. God never promises the end without knowing where to provide the means.' The man who demanded windows in heaven had forgotten that the God who once increased meal in a barrel needed no such thing to feed a city.

The witnesses stand together in the verdict. The scoffer's fault was not that he reasoned — his arithmetic of the improbable was sound enough — but that he made his own reason the boundary of God's power, and so called the promise incredible. That is the unbelief the repeated words are meant to condemn: not honest ignorance but the proud refusal to trust a word simply because one cannot see the means. And because the sin was signal, the sentence upon it was signal too; the very next verse (v.20) records, for the third time, how the man who limited the Holy One saw the abundance he had mocked and was trodden down before he could taste it — a warning, Henry says, that those who think themselves tantalized by the promises of God are justly tantalized by the promises of this world.
20:
The chapter ends where its threat pointed, and the witnesses read the third recital of the courtier's death as the sealing of the whole. 'And so it fell out unto him: for the people trode upon him in the gate, and he died.' Keil & Delitzsch and Clarke mark the exact correspondence of word and event; Clarke had already observed that such literal fulfilments won the prophet 'a great deal of credit in Israel,' and here the narrative closes the case by matching the prediction of v.2 against its outcome, syllable for syllable.

The puritan Matthew Henry gathers from the trampling his remaining lessons on the vanity of human standing and the certainty of divine threatening. The verse shows, he says, 'how uncertain life and the enjoyments of it are': 'honour and power cannot secure men from sudden and inglorious deaths.' He turns the courtier's very dignity into his epitaph — 'he whom the king leaned upon the people trod upon; he who fancied himself the stay and support of the government was trampled under foot as the mire in the streets.' And he draws the closing warning of 'how certain God's threatenings are, and how sure to alight on the guilty': 'let all men fear before the great God, who treads upon princes as mortar and is terrible to the kings of the earth.'

The witnesses do not divide; they let the repeated sentence stand as the chapter's final word. The man had scoffed at a promise and was denied its fruit; he had leaned at the king's right hand and was crushed by the feet of a crowd. In him the two threads of the story meet — the mercy that fed a starving city and the justice that answered contempt of God's word — and both are proved reliable to the letter. 'He that believeth not shall be damned,' Henry notes, 'stands as firm as He that believeth shall be saved.' So the deliverance of Samaria, promised in v.1 and fulfilled at the gate, leaves behind it this sober monument: that the same word which opens heaven's abundance to faith shuts the scoffer out from the very plenty he lives to see, and that no threat of God, any more than his promise, shall fall unaccomplished to the ground.
